summary: Jeff Atwood explores VIA's Nano-ITX motherboard format, which fits a complete PC into a remarkably small 12x12 centimeter footprint. He explains the development challenges including interference and heat problems that delayed the board's release. The post provides a comparison of various motherboard form factors from Nano-ITX up to standard ATX, along with pricing for the two available versions (fanless 800 MHz and fan-cooled 1 GHz). Atwood notes that while you could build a PC smaller than a Mac Mini using this board, the VIA CPUs offer modest performance suitable only for basic tasks.
key_points: Nano-ITX fits a complete motherboard into 12x12 cm, significantly smaller than Mini-ITX (17x17 cm) or standard ATX (30.5x24.4 cm)
Development delays were caused by interference and heat problems inherent to the extremely small form factor
Two versions available: fanless 800 MHz (~$300) and fan-cooled 1 GHz (~$350)
A complete mini-PC build would cost roughly $555-605 including drive, RAM, DVD, case and power supply
VIA CPUs are adequate for basic web browsing and productivity but lack power for demanding tasks
money_quotes: VIA's Nano-ITX fits an entire PC motherboard into an impossibly small 12 by 12 centimeter format.
It'd be easy to build a PC smaller than a Mac Mini using a Nano-ITX board.
Just don't expect a lot of power; the VIA CPUs aren't exactly barn-burners.
The VIA boards are fine for basic web browsing and productivity tasks with undemanding users.
key_insight: Extreme miniaturization in PC hardware involves significant engineering tradeoffs between size, heat management, and processing power.
